text: Despite the US-Iran deal, the SPR releases will continue for a while. Last week, DoE put ~8.89 million barrels into the market (~1.3m b/d), continuing with several weeks of >1m b/d releases that are helping to keep the American oil market better supplied.
Question will be whether or not that matters for Trump in the end.

Yet another US-sanctioned VLCC has infiltrated the US Navy blockade line while spoofing to show that she is still outside of it. The supertanker is currently loading 2 million barrels of Iranian crude oil. We can also say that we are seeing further tanker movements by NITC (National Iranian Tanker Company). Two VLCCs are currently heading further east; which places them closer to the blockade line, while another three are no longer at Iran's most eastern port of Chabahar. Their whereabouts are unknown for now. Something's happening.

#OOTT #IranWar #Tankers

Trump's deal with Iran looks like a return to the pre-war status quo, only with Iran in a stronger negotiating position - Bloomberg
Trump's Iran war is one of the greatest strategic blunders in US history. - Sky

CIA Director John Ratcliffe told Trump and other senior officials that intelligence gathered by the U.S. raised serious doubts about Iran's willingness to make the nuclear concessions the U.S. is seeking in a final deal - Axios

#Agriculture: In data going back to 2006, we have just witnessed the largest one-week reduction in managed money exposure to the 12 futures markets that make up the Bloomberg Commodity Agriculture Index. From a four-year high above one million net-long contracts just a month ago, the position collapsed to only 169,000 contracts in the week to 9 June. A week that saw money managers flip to net shorts in KC wheat and corn, the latter seeing its largest two-week selling burst on record. 

What makes the move particularly striking is that it occurred during a period when the index itself fell by "only" around 7%. The scale of the liquidation highlights a dramatic shift in sentiment, driven by a sharp reduction in weather-related risk premiums after conditions improved across several key growing regions, lifting the prospects for higher yields despite the recent fertilizer shortages. 
 
At the same time, falling energy prices weakened support from the biofuel sector, reducing demand expectations for crops linked to renewable fuel production. With oil markets increasingly pricing in the prospect of an interim Middle East peace deal announced this weekend, traders moved aggressively to unwind positions that only weeks ago had been built on concerns about supply risks partly driven by fertilizer shortages, adverse weather, and stronger biofuel demand.

While #gold, despite the dramatic sell-off, has so far suffered only what technicians would describe as a relatively shallow correction within a strong long-term uptrend, #platinum has experienced a much deeper setback. Weaker liquidity, combined with concerns about global economic growth and industrial demand, helped drive a correction that came close to derailing the broader bull trend.

Using the 2022–2026 rally as a reference, gold found support below USD 4,100 after retracing just 38.2% of its advance, highlighting the resilience of investor demand despite recent liquidation. Platinum, by contrast, endured a much deeper 61.8% retracement during last week's capitulation sell-off across precious metals and the PGM sector, underscoring both its higher volatility and thinner market liquidity.

Today, platinum is rebounding strongly, up 4.6%, with traders focusing on resistance at the recent breakdown zone and 0.50 retracement around USD 1,870, followed by the 200-day moving average at USD 1,906 - a key technical hurdle, which just like gold, may determine whether the latest rebound develops into a more sustained recovery.

Gold and other hard assets rallied strongly after the announcement of an interim peace deal, as easing concerns over energy prices helped reduce the inflation threat that has weighed heavily on the sector throughout the Middle East conflict. Lower oil prices have also prompted traders to scale back expectations for further interest-rate hikes, providing an additional tailwind for precious metals. Following last week's capitulation-style sell-off in both gold and silver, positioning has become considerably cleaner, leaving traders better placed to respond to shifts in either the technical or fundamental outlook. For gold, the key upside hurdle remains the 200-day moving average, currently near USD 4,450, a level that may determine whether the latest rebound develops into a more sustained recovery.

Wind Financial Morning Post: June 16, 2026

Market Brief

Trump: Details of the U.S.-Iran agreement will be disclosed after it is formally signed on June 19.

Israel says the U.S.-Iran agreement is “not binding” on Israel, and that Iran will not possess nuclear weapons.
Iran will charge fees for shipping services related to the Strait of Hormuz.

Global stock markets rallied on Monday: the Dow hit another record high, and China’s ChiNext Index rose more than 5%.

International oil prices fell more than 4% after the U.S. and Iran reached an agreement, while gold returned to the $4,300 level.

U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve falls to a nearly 43-year low, with plans to replenish 200 million barrels over the next year.

SpaceX’s valuation surpasses $2.5 trillion, with total IPO fundraising rising to $85.7 billion.

Nvidia issues investment-grade bonds for the first time in five years, planning to raise $25 billion.

Top Stories

The memorandum of understanding on a ceasefire reached between the United States and Iran has already been signed electronically, with a formal signing ceremony scheduled for June 19 in Geneva. Some provisions of the memorandum will begin taking effect on the 15th, while others, including the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, will take effect after the formal signing on the 19th. U.S. President Donald Trump said the contents of the U.S.-Iran agreement will be disclosed after the formal signing. Iran will be allowed to conduct limited, low-level uranium enrichment. Iran’s Foreign Ministry said asset unfreezing and war compensation are priorities in the memorandum, that Lebanon is an inseparable part of the Iran-U.S. ceasefire agreement, and that nuclear talks and sanctions relief will begin within 60 days after the memorandum is signed. In addition, Iran will charge fees for shipping services related to the Strait of Hormuz.

Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian said that, after intensive discussions, an overwhelming majority of members of Iran’s Islamic Consultative Assembly agreed to the text of the Iran-U.S. memorandum of understanding, as a practical test of the United States’ genuine determination to respect the rights of the Iranian people. Pezeshkian noted that the contents agreed in the memorandum are an important step toward ending the war and opening negotiations, but that a final agreement has not yet been reached. Iran is prepared for all possible options.

In response to the memorandum of understanding between the United States and Iran, Israeli National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir said the agreement reached by the U.S. and Iran is “not binding” on Israel. 

Israel would “never accept” anything short of the complete dismantling of Hezbollah in Lebanon, and Israel will not withdraw from occupied territories. Sources revealed that, during a phone call between Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and U.S. President Donald Trump, Netanyahu made clear that Israel does not consider itself bound by the Lebanon-related provisions in the U.S.-Iran agreement. Netanyahu also said that, with or without this agreement, Iran will never be allowed to possess nuclear weapons.

Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley, the underwriters for SpaceX’s IPO, exercised a “greenshoe” over-allotment option for 83.3 million shares, pushing SpaceX’s total fundraising to $85.7 billion and continuing to set new global IPO records. On Monday, SpaceX shares closed up 19.60%, sending its market capitalization above $2.5 trillion. SpaceX CEO Elon Musk said the company could reach $1 trillion in revenue by 2030.

China Macro & Policy

China’s State Council held its 20th special study session on the theme of “strengthening the implementation of the major function-oriented zoning strategy and promoting coordinated regional development.” Premier Li Qiang, while presiding over the session, stressed the need to further improve the major function-oriented zoning system, promote a new pattern of territorial spatial development and protection featuring clearly defined main functions, complementary strengths, and high-quality development, and provide strong support for advancing coordinated regional development and optimizing the layout of major productive forces.

The State Council’s work safety assessment and inspection teams recently visited eight provincial-level regions, including Hebei, Shanxi, Heilongjiang, Anhui, Hunan, Guangxi, Chongqing, and Xinjiang, to carry out strict, practical, and detailed open and covert inspections for the second and third quarters. The teams will take a serious and rigorous approach, confront problems head-on, crack down hard on all types of illegal and unlawful activities, and resolutely prevent and curb major and serious accidents.

The Hong Kong SAR Government has launched a public consultation on Hong Kong’s first five-year plan for economic and social development for 2026-2030, covering areas such as the economy, industries, spatial planning, infrastructure, green transition, as well as people’s livelihoods and well-being, including healthcare, education, housing, welfare, and elderly care.

China Equities Markets

China’s A-share market surged, with the Shanghai Composite Index closing up 1.61% at 4,096.47 points, the Shenzhen Component Index rising 3.79%, the ChiNext Index jumping 5.3%, the STAR 50 Index gaining 5.12%, and the Wind All A Index up 2.95%. Market turnover exceeded RMB 3 trillion. On the market, the AI industrial chain rallied across the board, with copper-clad laminates, printed circuit boards, optical modules, semiconductor silicon wafers, and HBM-related concepts leading the gains, while coal, banking, and oil and gas stocks were among the biggest decliners.

Hong Kong stocks closed higher after a volatile session. The Hang Seng Index rose 0.5% to 24,842.67 points, the Hang Seng Tech Index gained 1.28%, and the Hang Seng China Enterprises Index edged up 0.02%. AI application stocks led the gains, with Zhipu surging more than 32%. Semiconductors, hardware equipment, and non-ferrous metals were among the top-performing sectors, while coal stocks led the losses. Southbound funds recorded net buying of HKD 2.1 billion, with Zhipu seeing additional purchases of nearly HKD 2.3 billion.

Since June, institutional research interest in A-shares has heated up significantly, with funds actively positioning in high-quality market targets. Judging from research trends, high-growth sectors such as semiconductors and optoelectronics have attracted concentrated institutional visits. Since June, a total of 338 listed companies have received institutional research visits, among which 15 companies, including Jereh Group, Dajin Heavy Industry, Shanghai Hanxun, TCL Technology, and Rongda Photosensitive, were visited by 50 or more institutions. In terms of research topics, listed companies’ global expansion strategies have drawn strong institutional attention.

According to Wind data, as of June 15, nearly 600 companies had carried out share buybacks this year, with the total repurchase amount approaching RMB 58 billion. Among them, Midea Group had repurchased RMB 4.939 billion, the highest amount; BOE Technology Group A and SF Holding had repurchased RMB 4.729 billion and RMB 4.237 billion, respectively.

Two leading chip companies passed their IPO reviews on the same day. Information on the stock exchange websites showed that the IPOs of Enflame Technology and CanSemi Technology were both approved by the listing committee. Enflame Technology is one of China’s “four domestic GPU unicorns” and plans to list on the STAR Market, raising RMB 6 billion. CanSemi Technology plans to raise RMB 7.5 billion and is expected to become the first wafer manufacturer listed on the ChiNext Market.

Taiwan’s weighted index closed up 2.78% at 45,396.99 points. MediaTek rose nearly 7%, while TSMC and Hon Hai gained nearly 3%.

China Industry News

Five government departments have launched a three-year campaign to tackle energy-saving and carbon-reduction upgrades in key industries. Starting in 2026, comprehensive upgrades will be carried out across nine major energy-intensive industries: steel, electrolytic aluminum, cement, flat glass, oil refining, ethylene, synthetic ammonia, methanol, and coal-fired power. Capacity that still fails to meet energy-efficiency standards by the end of 2028 will be eliminated and shut down in accordance with laws and regulations.

China has, for the first time, successfully achieved independent mass production of silicon-28 isotopes with an abundance of more than 99.99%. Key product indicators have reached internationally advanced levels, which will support China’s independent development of core materials for silicon-based quantum computing, as well as frontier technology fields such as advanced-process semiconductors and high-end navigation.

MLCCs may be heading into the “longest shortage cycle in history.” According to reports, the MLCC shortage has begun to spread, with shortages expanding from high-end products to mid- and low-end categories. Suppliers planning capacity expansions are being constrained by equipment bottlenecks, and the industry expects the shortage may last until 2027 or even 2028.

According to the State Post Bureau, China’s express delivery development index stood at 472.9 in May, up 3.1% year on year. By sub-index, express delivery volume in May is estimated to have increased by around 6% year on year, while express delivery revenue is estimated to have grown by around 8.5%.

Fintech

Zhou Yanli, chairman of the special committee of the Zhongguancun Fintech Industry Development Alliance and former vice chairman of the China Insurance Regulatory Commission, recently said that regulators should, based on the trend of digital and intelligent transformation, improve fintech regulatory rules, clarify the boundaries of innovation and compliance requirements, avoid a “one-size-fits-all” regulatory approach, and leave reasonable room for innovative development.

The Philippine central bank has issued new regulatory guidelines for virtual assets, requiring all licensed virtual asset service providers to establish strict due diligence and asset review mechanisms before listing or trading any crypto asset. The new rules also explicitly prohibit “anonymity-enhanced virtual assets.”

Corporate News & Updates

Nvidia is returning to the investment-grade bond market after about five years. The company plans to raise $25 billion through the issuance of senior bonds. The offering attracted about $85 billion in orders, and the yield on the longest-dated tranche narrowed by 0.25 percentage points from initial price guidance, with the final spread set at 0.65 percentage points above U.S. Treasuries.

In response to a series of food safety issues recently identified by regulators and exposed by the media at Sam’s Club offline stores and online shops, China’s State Administration for Market Regulation summoned the head of Sam’s Club headquarters for a regulatory accountability meeting in accordance with the law. The regulator required the company to strictly prevent food safety risks across the entire chain and effectively safeguard public food safety.

Meta recently released an internal memo disclosing that the company expects spending on “internal AI use” alone to reach several billion dollars in 2026, and plans to formally implement a token management mechanism centered on budgets and quotas in 2027.

Fox Corporation will acquire streaming video platform Roku for about $22 billion. After the acquisition is completed, Fox will gain direct access to Roku’s massive existing user base of more than 100 million streaming households, helping it deliver targeted advertising more precisely and reduce its reliance on traditional TV distribution channels.

A Starbucks promotion in South Korea seriously backfired, leading to a significant decline in sales, and the CEO of Starbucks Korea was dismissed as a result. Starbucks Korea announced that it will close all stores nationwide at 3 p.m. local time on June 22 to conduct training for all employees.

Global Macro & Policy

The EU and Ukraine have formally launched the first negotiating chapter for Ukraine’s accession to the bloc. The EU and Ukraine officially began talks on the “fundamentals” cluster in Ukraine’s EU accession negotiations. According to the announcement, the “fundamentals” cluster covers the EU’s core values and basic principles, including EU laws and regulations in areas such as the rule of law and fundamental rights, the functioning of democratic institutions, public administration reform, and economic criteria.

French President Emmanuel Macron, responding to U.S. President Donald Trump’s tariff threat, said France will not bow to U.S. pressure and cancel its digital tax targeting American tech giants. Trump previously said the U.S. would impose a 100% tariff on French wine unless France scraps its digital tax on U.S. tech giants.

U.S. manufacturing output was flat month on month in May, ending four consecutive months of growth and falling short of market expectations. U.S. industrial production rose only 0.1% month on month in May, compared with expectations for a 0.3% increase, while the previous reading was revised to a 0.9% gain.

The Bank of Japan’s interest-rate decision is due today, with markets widely expecting the central bank to raise rates by 25 basis points to 1%. This would mark Japan’s first return to the 1% interest-rate era since 1995. At the same time, markets will closely watch for clues on when the BOJ may act again and monitor the yen’s performance.

European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde said high energy prices are spreading to other areas of the economy, with risks of “second-round effects,” especially wage increases, emerging. ECB Governing Council member Joachim Nagel said that even if the Strait of Hormuz resumes navigation soon, it will take months for oil supply to return to normal, and all options will remain open at the July meeting.

Turkey and Saudi Arabia plan to build a railway over the next three to four years connecting the two countries with Jordan and Syria, and extending directly to Europe. The railway is expected to help ease transportation difficulties caused by shipping disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz. The UAE, Kuwait, Qatar, Oman, Yemen, and other countries may also join the project in the future.

The South Korean government has officially launched a “Super Innovation Economy Project,” planning to invest KRW 500 billion in state funds to tackle next-generation power semiconductor technologies. Including matching private-sector funding, the total size of the R&D project is expected to expand to KRW 750 billion.

British Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced that people under the age of 16 will be banned from using certain social media platforms. Starmer said he hopes to pass the legislation before the end of the year, with the ban expected to take effect next spring.

Global Equity Markets

The three major U.S. stock indexes all closed higher. The Dow rose 0.92% to 51,671.03, setting another record high; the S&P 500 gained 1.65% to 7,554.29; and the Nasdaq climbed 3.07% to 26,683.94. Boeing rose more than 4% and Nvidia gained over 3%, leading the Dow. The Wind U.S. Magnificent Seven Index rose 2.71%, with Facebook up more than 4% and Amazon gaining over 3%. SpaceX surged 19.6%, lifting its total market capitalization to $2.52 trillion. The Nasdaq Golden Dragon China Index rose 0.39%, with Silvercorp Metals up more than 8% and Autohome gaining over 6%.

The three major European stock indexes ended mixed. Germany’s DAX rose 1.05% to 24,894.01; France’s CAC 40 gained 0.40% to 8,384.01; and the UK’s FTSE 100 fell 0.39% to 10,430.62. A temporary agreement between the U.S. and Iran to end the war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz boosted market risk appetite. However, the energy sector was dragged down by a sharp drop in oil prices, with oil stocks such as Shell and BP declining, putting pressure on the FTSE 100.

Major Asia-Pacific stock indexes closed higher. South Korea’s KOSPI rose 5.2% to 8,545.98, triggering circuit breakers for the sixth consecutive trading day. Japan’s Nikkei 225 gained 4.99% to 69,317.5, closing above 69,000 for the first time. AI-related stocks rallied across the board, with Kioxia rising nearly 12%, SoftBank Group gaining more than 10%, Samsung Electronics up 4.5%, and SK Hynix climbing over 6%. India’s SENSEX 30 rose 0.97% to 76,264.33.

Data from the Bank of Korea showed that foreign securities investment funds recorded a net outflow of $26.15 billion in May, marking the fourth consecutive month of net outflows. Equity funds saw a net outflow of $31.83 billion, a record high for a single month. Since the beginning of this year, total foreign outflows from the stock market have reached $77.83 billion.

Bonds

China’s interbank bond market was largely range-bound, with yields on major interest-rate bonds mixed. Bonds with maturities of five years and above remained resilient, while those with maturities of one to three years were relatively weak. Main government bond futures contracts all rose, with the 30-year contract up 0.25%. Liquidity conditions in the interbank market tightened further. The central bank conducted RMB 425 billion in reverse repo operations, resulting in a net injection of RMB 206.5 billion.

Data from the PBOC Shanghai Head Office showed that as of the end of May 2026, overseas institutions held RMB 3.21 trillion of bonds in China’s interbank market, accounting for about 1.8% of the total custody volume in the interbank bond market.

U.S. Treasury yields were mixed. The 2-year Treasury yield fell 1.25 basis points to 4.066%, the 3-year yield fell 2.24 basis points to 4.114%, the 5-year yield fell 1.76 basis points to 4.190%, the 10-year yield fell 1.19 basis points to 4.473%, while the 30-year yield rose 0.90 basis point to 4.979%.

Commodities

The Shanghai Gold Exchange issued a notice stating that, from the close-of-day clearing on June 17, the margin ratio for contracts including Au(T+D) will be adjusted from 15% to 16%, and the daily price limit will be adjusted from 14% to 15% starting from the next trading day. For the Ag(T+D) contract, the margin ratio will be adjusted from 21% to 22%, and the daily price limit will be adjusted from 20% to 21% starting from the next trading day.

International precious metals futures broadly closed higher. COMEX gold futures rose 2.18% to $4,331 per ounce, while COMEX silver futures gained 3.07% to $70.06 per ounce. The U.S.-Iran peace agreement eased geopolitical risks and inflation concerns, while central bank gold purchases and loose liquidity also provided support.

International oil prices fell across the board. The main WTI crude contract dropped 4.38% to $81.16 per barrel, while the main Brent crude contract fell 4.55% to $83.36 per barrel. The U.S.-Iran peace agreement significantly eased the geopolitical risk premium. Combined with a weak global demand outlook and market concerns over oversupply, multiple bearish factors drove a pullback in oil prices.

Most London base metals rose. LME tin futures gained 2.97% to $55,350.0 per tonne, LME copper rose 0.72% to $13,796.5 per tonne, LME lead edged up 0.13% to $1,968.5 per tonne, and LME zinc inched up 0.01% to $3,584.5 per tonne. LME nickel fell 0.08% to $17,815.0 per tonne, while LME aluminum dropped 4.30% to $3,383.0 per tonne.

The Trump administration is accelerating the implementation of a 172-million-barrel crude oil release plan to hedge against the oil price surge caused by the U.S.-Iran war, pushing U.S. emergency crude supplies to their lowest level since 1983. Data from the U.S. Department of Energy showed that the Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR), established during the Arab oil embargo of the 1970s, has fallen to about 340 million barrels, approaching a record low. The Department of Energy plans to replenish about 200 million barrels over the next year, around 20% more than the current release volume.

The London Bullion Market Association (LBMA) is considering moving its morning gold auction earlier to better accommodate Asian traders. Currently, the LBMA Gold Price is set twice daily through an electronic platform, at 10:30 and 15:00 London time, and is denominated in U.S. dollars. The price serves as a benchmark used by global miners, refiners, investors, and central banks.

Singapore will launch an over-the-counter (OTC) gold clearing system this year and will also provide vault custody services for central banks, aiming to build itself into a global precious metals trading hub. Several major international banks, including JPMorgan Chase, Deutsche Bank, and DBS Bank, will participate in the system.

Citi raised its three-month gold price forecast to $4,500 per ounce and its silver price forecast to $70 per ounce. Citi lowered its oil price expectations to its previous bearish-case levels, with its latest quarterly oil price forecasts for the third and fourth quarters of 2026 at $75 and $70 per barrel, respectively.

FX & Currency Markets

On Monday, the onshore yuan closed at 6.7572 against the U.S. dollar at 16:30, up 44 basis points from the previous trading day and reaching its highest level since early February 2023. It closed at 6.7568 in night trading. The yuan’s central parity rate against the dollar was set at 6.8088, up 21 basis points from the previous trading day.

In late New York trading, the U.S. Dollar Index fell 0.13% to 99.67. Most non-U.S. currencies rose: the euro gained 0.20% against the dollar to 1.1590, sterling rose 0.05% to 1.3413, and the Australian dollar climbed 0.38% to 0.7070. The dollar rose 0.08% against the yen to 160.3540 and gained 0.02% against the Canadian dollar to 1.3991, while it fell 0.30% against the Swiss franc to 0.7947. The offshore yuan rose 45 basis points against the dollar to 6.7596.

China’s foreign exchange market operated generally steadily in May. Non-bank sectors, including enterprises and individuals, recorded net cross-border capital inflows of $62.5 billion, up slightly by 1% month on month. Banks recorded a foreign exchange settlement and sales surplus of $35.8 billion, down 11% month on month. Foreign investors were overall net buyers of domestic stocks and bonds.

South Korea’s Vice Finance Minister in charge of foreign exchange policy, Moon Jisung, recently met with U.S. Treasury officials to consult on foreign exchange market trends. Moon said the recent depreciation of the Korean won lacks reasonable support and has gone too far. The two sides agreed to work together to address won weakness.

Key Economic Events

Bank of Japan announces interest-rate decision
China Academy of Information and Communications Technology holds a seminar and launches the High-Quality Token Service Capability Climbing Plan
The 2026 3rd AEPT Solid-State Battery Summit and Starting Point Solid-State Battery Forum will be held from June 16 to 18

Sources: Glassnode · BofA · Goldman Sachs · CertIK

🌅 StockKit Morning Report — Monday, June 15, 2026
🌙 Overnight Futures
S&P +1.3% · Dow +1.0% · Nasdaq +2.1%
 — 
Stock futures soared and oil prices tumbled as Wall Street reacted to a deal to end U.S.-Iran hostilities and reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
📈 Yesterday's Close
Dow +0.7% (51,202) · S&P +0.5% (7,431) · Nasdaq +0.3% (25,889)
 — 
Stocks rose as SpaceX's opening pop bolstered sentiment, with investors hoping for a peace deal between the U.S. and Iran.
 
Goldman Sachs +2.6%, JPMorgan +2.3% led; Nike -2.2%, Apple -1.5% lagged.
 
SpaceX (SPCX) closed up 19% at $161 in its record-setting debut.
⚠️ The Story Continues
President Trump said late Sunday a deal with Iran was "now complete." Pakistan's PM said an official signing ceremony would take place Friday in Switzerland.
 
Oil cratered overnight — WTI down 5.5% to $80.20, Brent down 5.2% to $82.80
 — 
but a return to normal Strait of Hormuz flows is seen as months away.
 Inflation remains the wildcard: 
Wholesale inflation rose sharply last month as the Iran oil shock drove up business costs. Inflation is the worst in three years.
Earlier this year, many investors expected the Fed would begin cutting rates by mid-2026. Rising geopolitical tensions and higher energy prices have altered those expectations. Markets are now shifting toward the possibility the Fed may need to hold rates higher for longer, or potentially consider additional hikes.
 
Meanwhile, the 52nd G7 Summit kicks off today in Evian-les-Bains, France, hosted by Macron
 — expect headlines on Ukraine, trade fragmentation, and energy security.
With SpaceX's IPO done, two other mega-IPOs loom: OpenAI and Anthropic. Some investors are asking whether the market has the liquidity to absorb all this new supply, especially as hyperscaler stock buybacks have slowed dramatically.
 
Markets closed Friday for Juneteenth — this is a four-day trading week.
📅 Today
NY Empire State Manufacturing Index (8:30am)
 · 
SpaceX IPO expected to close.
 · 
34 earnings reports scheduled.
⚠️ Bottom Line
The Iran deal is the best news markets have had in weeks, and the reflex rally is understandable — but don't chase it. 
Talks on a final deal are "expected to begin within 60 days," and Hormuz normalization is months away.
 Oil's drop helps the inflation picture, but the damage is already baked into recent data. With mega-IPO supply still coming and the Fed boxed in by sticky prices, this rally has legs only if earnings can back it up. Stay nimble. Favor value and dividend payers over extended-multiple tech until the IPO calendar clears.
Always check the news before you buy. Check the Consensus Analyst Price Target — prefer stocks trading below it. The decision to buy is yours.
Despite the global news, Happy Trading!

Goldman Sachs expects Persian Gulf oil exports to return to pre-war levels by end-July, one month earlier than previously forecast.

Goldman cuts its oil price target for the second time in a week:

"We reduce our oil price forecast following President Trump's announcement of an interim deal that would lift the US blockade, and reopen the Strait of Hormuz after a scheduled deal signing on Friday. While full details on the agreement are unclear, we now assume that Persian Gulf exports normalize to pre-war levels by the end of July (vs. end of August previously). We are reducing our 2026Q4 Brent forecast to $80 (vs. $90 previously) and to $75 (vs. $80 prior) for the 2027 average"
Goldman Sachs says it now expects Persian Gulf oil exports to return to pre-war levels by end-July, up from a prior forecast of end-August. $SPY $USO

@Coinmatik1 Solid reminder on market cycles and a good breakdown of the March 2026 gold correction - the oil spike from US-Iran tensions, inflation fears, and delayed rate cuts did weigh on gold as described.

One clarification: Gold’s all-time high was actually in late January 2026 (~$5,589/oz), not March. By the March 22 post, it had already corrected significantly to ~$4,490. As of mid-June 2026 it’s trading around $4,300/oz.

Long-term bullish views on gold are common, but $7k–$9k in 2–3 years is quite optimistic/speculative vs. many bank forecasts (~$5k–$6k range by end-2026/2027).

Cycles matter - no asset rises forever. Appreciate the chart and analysis!

Will Nifty finally break 24,000 today or is this a trap?

The market opened strong again this morning, with Nifty comfortably crossing the 23,900 mark right out of the gate.
The main reason for the pump? The US-Iran ceasefire deal is officially moving markets. It completely crushed global Brent crude oil prices back down to $83, which is massive news for our economy and inflation.

But here is the catch. Every time Nifty gets close to that psychological 24,000 level, heavy profit booking kicks in. Traders are quietly cashing out at the top.

If you look closely at the live action today, smart money is currently dumping metal stocks and rotating straight into IT and FMCG. Do not blindly chase this morning's gap-up. Let the market digest the geopolitical news and see if these high levels actually sustain after lunch.

Are you buying anything today or just holding your portfolio?

quotedTweet.text:
The war might end this week. 

But the $58 billion in oil infrastructure damage will not.

Oil is trading at $80 after crashing 5% on the peace deal announcement. 

The war injected a 78% premium into oil prices, taking it from $67 before the war started to a peak of $119 on April 7. 

The 1991 Gulf War is the closest historical comparison. That conflict injected a 135% war premium. 

The entire premium was erased within 5 months of the peak as the market priced in peace before it was formally declared. If the same pattern plays out, the pre-war level of $67 is where oil is ultimately headed.

But the 1991 comparison stops there. 
In 1991, the infrastructure was largely intact when the war ended. The Strait reopened, supply came back, and prices normalised.

That is not what is happening in 2026.

The IEA chief confirmed that more than 80 energy facilities across the Middle East were attacked during this war. 

More than 40 of those are severely or very severely damaged across nine countries. Total damage is estimated at $58 billion by Rystad Energy. The minimum repair bill is $34 billion. 

The IEA's own estimate is that restoring oil and gas production to pre-war levels could take up to two years.

Oil pipelines, refineries, and gas processing plants are not like light switches. They cannot be turned back on because a piece of paper was signed in Geneva. 

Production systems require careful pressure management from deep underground reservoirs. 

Equipment needs to be inspected and certified safe before restart. Workers need to return to facilities that were recently under attack. Insurance markets do not immediately return to cover a region that was at war last week. 

Shipping companies do not immediately send tankers back through the Strait of Hormuz.

The Strait reopens within 30 days under the MOU. But the physical supply that was supposed to flow through it will take months to years to come back at full capacity.

This matters for inflation directly. 

US CPI hit 4.2% in May, the third consecutive monthly acceleration, with energy driving more than 60% of that increase. A peace deal stops the war. 

It does not repair a refinery. It does not fix a pipeline. It does not rebuild LNG infrastructure. The energy supply disruption that drove inflation higher does not immediately reverse because the shooting stopped.

The war premium in oil will unwind. 

The chart and every historical precedent confirm that. But the underlying supply damage means energy inflation does not unwind at the same speed. 

That gap between oil prices falling and energy supply actually recovering, is where the inflation problem sits for the next 12 to 24 months.

Mid-Day Markets - June 15, 2026

This Morning's Top Story

The macro landscape has shifted dramatically into a full risk-off liquidation cycle today, June 15, 2026, as geopolitical stress completely deflates across global desks. Rumors of a structured resolution in the Middle East gulf have solidified into a tangible peace agreement framework. While official texts remain tightly held, capital markets have waste no time pricing in the immediate de-escalation: crude oil has broken sharply below its previous spring baselines, and commercial shipping channels are preparing to resume normal transit through critical global chokepoints.

This aggressive removal of the global inflation premium is clashing directly with a highly productive domestic weather setup across the U.S. Corn Belt. Last week’s widespread regional rain systems have given way to dry, warming early-summer conditions that favor rapid crop development. With speculative fund managers systematically cutting back long positions ahead of this afternoon's weekly USDA Crop Progress report, front-month agricultural paper is facing an intense technical wash out, dragging the commodity complex down toward major psychological support baselines.

Market News (Performance Review & Rebalancing)

Early-session fund rebalancing is fast-tracking a broad macro retreat, forcing both row crops and livestock to recalibrate to shifting supply baselines.

Crude Oil Plummets on Peace News: WTI crude futures are leading the macro decline today, crashing down to trade near $84.80 per barrel. The sudden collapse of regional hostilities and expectations of normalizing ocean traffic have completely vaporized the geopolitical war premium, dragging down broader energy-linked agricultural margins—most notably across the domestic ethanol and biofuel complexes.

Grains Enter New Low Territories: The row-crop complex is trading with an incredibly heavy tone as near-perfect growing conditions erase summer weather premiums. July Corn has broken critical technical support, sliding down to $4.08 per bushel at mid-session despite a relatively neutral June WASDE print that left baseline demand projections unchanged. July Soybeans have followed the downward slide, trading soft at $11.08 as improved midwestern moisture models overshadow minor storm and wind damage in localized pockets of Iowa.

Livestock Hits a Wall: The meat complex has run into a severe technical barrier at mid-session, completely stalling out early-morning momentum to trade lower. August Live Cattle are under pressure near $239.50, while the CME Feeder Index has pulled back to $368.01. The corrective action follows a highly restricted weekly slaughter of just 524,000 head one of the lowest non-holiday volumes seen in a long time proving packers are actively cutting operations to revive retail box beef prices. 

At the same time, analytical consensus indicates that while the Texas screwworm crisis slows herd rebuilding, it will not derail the underlying high-weight structure of current feedyard inventories.
Key Levels (The Map)

Grains

Corn (/ZC): Support 400 - Resistance 420 - Status: Sharp Decline; July contract breaking below previous support to $4.08 as the combination of lower crude oil and cooperative weather isolates bulls.

Soybeans (/ZS): Support 1100 - Resistance 1135 - Status: Soft; July beans falling to $11.08, testing major psychological support as regional moisture runs remain highly favorable.

Wheat (/ZW): Support 570 - Resistance 600 - Status: Steady to Lower; July Chicago wheat holding at $5.84, attempting to establish a baseline even as winter harvest progress tops 10% in core areas.

Livestock
Live Cattle (/LE): Support 238.00 - Resistance 242.00 - Status: Technical Reversal; August contracts losing grip on early gains to trade at $239.50 as restricted packer slaughter volumes limit near-term cash bids.

Lean Hogs (/HE): Support 93.00 - Resistance 97.00 - Status: Firming; Lean hog equivalents show solid cash premium jumps, keeping front-month contracts supported near 95.80 cents.

Energy & Metals
Crude Oil (/CL): Support 82.00 - Resistance 86.00 - Status: Heavy Liquidation; WTI crude sliding to $84.88 as the return of open gulf shipping structures dries up speculative energy weights.

Gold (/GC): Support 4350 - Resistance 4420 - Status: Quietly Lower; Feeling minor pressure as the sudden drop in geopolitical risks reduces systemic safe-haven demand.

Chart of the Day: July Corn (/CN26) Technical Setup
Action: Enforce Short Hedges / Defer Unpriced Old-Crop Sales.

Why: July Corn has breached its structural defensive boundary, tumbling to a contract low of $4.08. Seasonality trends typically peak around mid-June, but the combination of a massive 183 bu/acre trendline yield baseline and the complete collapse of energy-linked inflation has triggered heavy institutional long-liquidation.

Tactical Note: The contract is rapidly descending toward the critical $4.00 psychological shelf. Until automated CTA models signal that net-short positioning has reached complete exhaustion, any temporary intraday bounces should be viewed as resistance retests rather than structural trend shifts.

Market Pulse & Strategy
The smart money is moving with aggressive seasonal efficiency today. Automated asset managers are aggressively dumping long commodity exposure to adjust for the structural shift in global crude transport. With row-crop planting fully finalized across the Midwest and early-season emergence running at a clean pace, systematic capital is abandoning risk premiums entirely. Commercial buyers are retreating to the sidelines, content to let the market bleed lower until a genuine high-summer heat or dryness threat emerges on the weather maps.

Pro Takeaway: The entire landscape has reset. The fact that packers dropped slaughter volumes down to 524,000 head proves they have zero intention of chasing high-priced live cattle while wholesale margins are tight. Do not misinterpret the ongoing screwworm headlines as an immediate reason to abandon your protection—manage your livestock hedges with extreme care because near-term packer demand is highly restricted.

Mode

The bears are completely driving the bus in the grain room today. Seeing July Corn crack down to $4.08 tells you everything you need to know about what happens when perfect early-season weather collides with a massive drop in crude oil. The bulls have run out of arguments, and everyone is bracing for a highly uniform initial condition score on the afternoon crop progress tape. Over in the cattle complex, the screen ran straight into a wall today. Packers are playing defense by letting the kill numbers drop to non-holiday lows, keeping a firm lid on what feedlots can secure in the country. Keep your defensive strategy completely intact.

Notes

The Slaughter Squeeze: The dramatic reduction in weekly slaughter volume down to 524,000 head 36,000 head below last year's pace is a major warning shot for feedlots. Packers are successfully using reduced operations to artificially support box beef prices without having to pay up for physical cash cattle. 

With feedlot inventories still carrying high average weights, this operational slowdown transfers all intermediate holding pressure directly back onto the producer.

The Rest of the Week

Monday, June 15 (4:00 PM Eastern): USDA Weekly Crop Progress report. This print will deliver highly scrutinized updates on early-season row-crop conditions across the Corn Belt.

Wednesday, June 17: Regular weekly EIA crude and ethanol inventory data. Watch closely to see how hard the crude oil price drop hits domestic ethanol blending volumes.

Trader Strategy: Hold aggressive trailing stops on all remaining old-crop grain layers, recognizing that a breach of $4.00 corn could quickly fast-track automated fund selling. In livestock, treat any minor technical recovery as a strategic opportunity to reinforce baseline hedges, keeping your downside safety nets closely tied to the reality of reduced packer slaughter schedules.

FITCH: REOPENING THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ COULD LOWER OIL PRICES AND REDUCE CREDIT RISKS

According to Fitch Ratings, a potential agreement between the United States and Iran, combined with the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, could significantly reduce one of the most important risks currently priced into global markets.

Key takeaways from Fitch's assessment:

▪️ Oil exports and shipping flows could normalize within weeks.
▪️ The global oil market could return to a supply surplus within roughly one month.
▪️ Limited damage to regional energy infrastructure would support a rapid recovery in production.
▪️ Rising non-OPEC supply and available spare OPEC capacity could add further downward pressure on oil prices.
Fitch continues to forecast an average Brent crude price of $87 per barrel for 2026 but notes that an earlier-than-expected reopening of the Strait of Hormuz would create downside risks to that outlook.
However, Fitch also emphasizes that lower energy market tensions do not mean geopolitical risks have disappeared.
Iran's nuclear program, Israel's potential response, regional proxy conflicts, and U.S. domestic politics are all expected to remain key drivers of Middle East risk premiums in the years ahead.

The next phase for global markets may not be about oil shortages, but rather about how investors reassess the region's long-term security and geopolitical balance.

#Oil #Brent #HormuzStrait #EnergyMarkets #FitchRatings #MiddleEast #Geopolitics #Commodities #Investing #Macro
